Read the following passage carefully. There will be questions at the end.

The Kepler space telescope, launched by NASA on March 7, 2009, was designed to survey a portion of the Milky Way galaxy to discover Earth-size exoplanets in or near habitable zones and estimate how many of the billions of stars in the Milky Way have such planets. Named after the German astronomer Johannes Kepler, it observed 530,506 stars and detected 2,662 exoplanets over its operational lifetime. The spacecraft operated from an Earth-trailing heliocentric orbit and used a photometer that continuously monitored the brightness of over 150,000 main sequence stars in a fixed field of view.

The telescope had a 0.95-meter aperture Schmidt camera design with a 105 square degree field of view, much larger than any previous space telescope. Its CCD array contained 42 CCDs with 2200x1024 pixels each, totaling over 95 million pixels. The mission cost approximately $600 million over its lifetime.

Kepler detected planets using the transit method, observing tiny periodic dips in stellar brightness when a planet crosses in front of its host star. A typical Earth-like planet transiting a Sun-like star would cause a brightness reduction of about 84 parts per million. The telescope needed to observe at least three transits to confirm a planetary candidate.

The original mission ended in May 2013 when a second of four reaction wheels failed, preventing precise pointing. NASA repurposed the spacecraft as K2, which observed different patches of sky along the ecliptic plane. K2 discovered an additional 529 confirmed exoplanets before the spacecraft ran out of fuel and was retired on October 30, 2018.

Questions:
1. How many stars did Kepler observe in total?
2. What was the total pixel count of its CCD array?
3. How many reaction wheels needed to fail before the original mission ended?
4. How many confirmed exoplanets did K2 (the repurposed mission) discover?
5. What date was the spacecraft retired?

Summarize the following technical specification in exactly 3 bullet points:

The XR-7000 Industrial Controller supports up to 256 discrete I/O channels, 64 analog input channels (16-bit resolution, ±10V range, 100kHz aggregate sampling rate), and 32 analog output channels (14-bit resolution, ±10V range). Communication interfaces include dual Gigabit Ethernet ports with EtherCAT support (cycle time down to 125μs), one RS-485 serial port (up to 12Mbps), and an optional CAN bus module (CAN 2.0B, up to 1Mbps). The controller runs a real-time Linux kernel (PREEMPT_RT patch) with guaranteed worst-case interrupt latency of 50μs. Internal storage consists of 32GB eMMC for the OS and application code, plus a removable SD card slot (up to 512GB) for data logging. The unit operates from 24VDC (±20%) input power, consuming 45W typical and 78W maximum. Operating temperature range is -20°C to +60°C with no derating. The chassis is IP67 rated and weighs 2.8kg. MTBF is rated at 150,000 hours per MIL-HDBK-217F.

The following paragraph contains exactly ONE false statement. Identify it.

The Great Wall of China stretches over 13,000 miles and was built over many centuries beginning in the 7th century BC. The wall is visible from the International Space Station with the naked eye under favorable conditions. The primary purpose of the wall was military defense against invasions from the north. Construction materials varied by region, including tamped earth, brick, stone, and wood. The wall was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987. During the Ming Dynasty (1368-1644), the wall underwent significant reconstruction and expansion. Several sections of the wall are now popular tourist destinations, with the Badaling section near Beijing being the most visited.

Below is a passage with key numbers embedded throughout. Read it and answer the numerical questions at the end.

The city of Avalonia was founded in 1847 by 342 settlers who arrived on 7 ships. By 1900, the population had grown to 15,847. The city's main bridge, completed in 1923, spans 1,247 feet across the Emerald River and cost $3.2 million to build (approximately $56.8 million in today's dollars). The bridge has 4 main support pillars, each sunk 89 feet into the riverbed.

The Avalonia Public Library, established in 1891 with a donation of $175,000 from industrialist Marcus Webb, currently houses 847,000 volumes across 6 floors. The building was renovated in 1967, 2001, and most recently in 2019 at a cost of $12.4 million. The library employs 156 staff members and serves approximately 23,000 active cardholders.

The city's annual Harvest Festival, first held in 1853, attracts an average of 45,000 visitors over its 3-day duration. The festival features 127 food vendors, 34 live music acts, and generates approximately $2.7 million in revenue for local businesses. The festival's famous pumpkin contest has been won 8 times by the Henderson family.

Questions:
1. How many ships brought the original settlers?
2. What was the bridge construction cost in today's dollars?
3. How deep are the bridge support pillars?
4. When was the library's most recent renovation?
5. How much revenue does the Harvest Festival generate?

Read the following recipe and answer questions.

Classic Beef Bourguignon (Serves 6)

Ingredients:
- 3 lbs chuck beef, cut into 2-inch cubes
- 6 oz bacon lardons
- 1 bottle (750ml) Burgundy red wine
- 2 cups beef stock
- 1 tablespoon tomato paste
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 teaspoon thyme
- 2 bay leaves
- 18 pearl onions, peeled
- 1 lb cremini mushrooms, quartered
- 3 carrots, cut into 1-inch pieces
- 2 tablespoons flour
- 3 tablespoons butter
- 2 tablespoons olive oil
- Salt and pepper to taste
- Fresh parsley for garnish

Instructions:
1. Pat beef dry. Season with salt and pepper.
2. In a Dutch oven, cook bacon lardons over medium heat for 8 minutes until crispy. Remove and set aside.
3. In bacon fat, brown beef in batches (don't crowd the pan). 3-4 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.
4. Sauté carrots and pearl onions for 5 minutes. Add garlic, cook 1 minute.
5. Sprinkle flour over vegetables, stir for 2 minutes.
6. Add wine, scraping the bottom. Add stock, tomato paste, thyme, and bay leaves.
7. Return beef and bacon to pot. Bring to simmer.
8. Cover and transfer to 325°F oven for 2.5-3 hours.
9. During last 30 minutes, sauté mushrooms in 2 tablespoons butter until golden (about 8 minutes).
10. Remove bay leaves. Stir in mushrooms. Adjust seasoning.
11. Garnish with parsley. Serve over egg noodles or mashed potatoes.

Questions:
1. How many pearl onions are needed?
2. What temperature is the oven set to?
3. How long should the bacon cook?
4. At what step do you add the mushrooms to the main pot?
5. How much wine is used?

Here are descriptions of 8 fictional programming languages. Answer the questions by matching languages to requirements.

CrystalScript: Statically typed, compiles to native code, manual memory management, excellent C interop, syntax similar to Ruby. Best for: systems programming, game engines. Weaknesses: steep learning curve, no garbage collector.

FlowLang: Dynamically typed, interpreted, built-in actor model for concurrency, pattern matching, hot code reloading. Best for: distributed systems, telecom. Weaknesses: slow single-threaded performance, small ecosystem.

Verdant: Statically typed, garbage collected, first-class functions, built-in package manager, compiles to WebAssembly and native. Best for: full-stack web development. Weaknesses: young ecosystem, breaking changes between versions.

EchoML: Statically typed with type inference, purely functional, algebraic data types, proof assistant integration. Best for: financial systems, formal verification. Weaknesses: hard to hire developers, poor I/O performance.

Blitz: Dynamically typed, JIT compiled, NumPy-like array primitives built into the language, GPU kernel support. Best for: data science, machine learning. Weaknesses: not suitable for long-running services, memory-hungry.

Arcane: Gradually typed, interpreted with optional AOT compilation, Lisp-like macro system, REPL-driven development. Best for: prototyping, tooling, scripting. Weaknesses: inconsistent performance, Lisp syntax polarizing.

Nexus: Statically typed, async/await as core primitives, built-in HTTP server, capability-based security model. Best for: microservices, APIs. Weaknesses: no GUI libraries, limited embedded use.

Forge: Statically typed, borrow checker for memory safety without GC, algebraic effects, cross-compilation to 30+ targets. Best for: embedded systems, CLI tools. Weaknesses: long compile times, complex type system.

Questions:
1. Which language is best for a team building a high-frequency trading platform that needs formal correctness guarantees?

3. Which language would you pick for a rapid prototype of a developer CLI tool?

PR #347: Add rate limiting to API gateway
Author: @sarah-dev

@sarah-dev (Mar 5, 10:02 AM):
This PR adds token bucket rate limiting to the API gateway. Default is 100 req/min per API key. Configurable via env vars. Tests included.

@mike-lead (Mar 5, 2:15 PM):
Nice work overall. A few concerns:
1. Line 45: The token refill uses time.time() which isn't monotonic. Use time.monotonic() instead.

4. Missing: No test for concurrent access — race conditions are the #1 issue with rate limiters.

@sarah-dev (Mar 5, 4:30 PM):

Looks much better. One more thing: the connection pool size should be configurable (env var), not hardcoded at 50. Other than that, LGTM. Approve with that minor fix.

A fictional research paper abstract and introduction are below. Answer comprehension questions.

Title: Adaptive Precision Routing in Mixture-of-Experts Inference
Authors: Zhang, Wei; Okafor, Chidi; Larsson, Erik
Venue: Proceedings of SysML 2026

Abstract: We present APR-MoE, a runtime system that dynamically assigns bit-widths to individual experts in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models based on per-token routing confidence. When the gating network assigns a token to an expert with high confidence (softmax probability > 0.85), that expert runs at INT4 precision. For medium-confidence routing (0.45-0.85), experts execute at INT8. Low-confidence routing (< 0.45) triggers FP16 fallback. Evaluated on Mixtral-8x22B across 6 benchmarks, APR-MoE achieves 2.3x throughput improvement over uniform FP16 inference with only 1.2% average accuracy degradation. On reasoning-heavy tasks (GSM8K, MATH), accuracy loss is 2.8%, while on knowledge retrieval (TriviaQA, NaturalQuestions), loss is only 0.3%. The system adds 0.4ms overhead per token for precision routing decisions. We release APR-MoE as an open-source vLLM plugin.

Introduction (excerpt): The key insight behind APR-MoE is that not all expert activations are equally important. When a gating network routes a token to an expert with 95% confidence, the computation within that expert is highly constrained — small precision errors are unlikely to change the output. Conversely, when routing confidence is low (e.g., 30%), the gating network is uncertain which expert is correct, making precision-sensitive computation critical for maintaining output quality. Prior work on mixed-precision MoE inference (DynaExq, MiLo) assigns static precision per expert based on offline calibration. APR-MoE is the first system to make precision decisions dynamically at the per-token level during inference.

2. On which task category does APR-MoE lose the most accuracy?
3. What is the per-token overhead of the precision routing?
4. How does APR-MoE differ from DynaExq and MiLo?
5. What model was used for evaluation?

Below is a troubleshooting decision tree for a network issue. Follow the logic and answer.

Problem: User reports 'Cannot connect to internal application at app.internal.corp'

Step 1: Can you ping 8.8.8.8?
  YES → Go to Step 2
  NO → Check physical connection. Is cable plugged in?
    YES → Check if NIC is enabled in OS. Go to Step 6.
    NO → Plug in cable. Problem solved? If NO → replace cable.

Step 2: Can you ping app.internal.corp by hostname?
  YES → Go to Step 3
  NO → Can you ping the app's IP address 10.50.2.100 directly?
    YES → DNS issue. Check /etc/resolv.conf or DNS server settings. Internal DNS should be 10.50.1.10.
    NO → Go to Step 4

Step 3: Can you reach the application in a browser (HTTPS on port 443)?
  YES → Problem is intermittent or resolved. Monitor.
  NO → Check if port 443 is open: run 'telnet app.internal.corp 443'
    CONNECTED → Application server issue. Check server logs. Escalate to Tier 2.
    REFUSED → Firewall rule blocking traffic. Check user's VLAN assignment. Users on VLAN 200 need firewall rule FW-347 enabled.

Step 4: Run traceroute to 10.50.2.100. Does it stop at the gateway (10.50.1.1)?
  YES → Gateway/routing issue. Check if the route to 10.50.2.0/24 exists. Contact network team.
  NO → Does it stop at the firewall (10.50.1.5)?
    YES → Firewall blocking. Check ACL rules for user's source IP.
    NO → Contact network team with traceroute output.

Step 6: Is NIC showing 'Media disconnected'?
  YES → Driver issue. Reinstall NIC driver, version 4.2.1 from internal repo.
  NO → Check IP configuration. Is DHCP assigned? If static, verify subnet mask and gateway.

Scenario: A user can ping 8.8.8.8 successfully. They cannot ping app.internal.corp by name, but CAN ping 10.50.2.100. What is the diagnosis and fix?

Architecture Overview:
1. Client sends HTTPS request to api.example.com
2. AWS CloudFront CDN terminates TLS (edge location)
3. CloudFront forwards to Application Load Balancer (ALB) on port 443
4. ALB routes based on path:
   - /api/v1/* → ECS Service 'api-gateway' (3 tasks, Fargate)
   - /api/v2/* → EKS Pod 'api-gateway-v2' (5 replicas)
   - /static/* → S3 bucket 'assets-prod'
5. API Gateway service:
   - Validates JWT token against Auth0
   - Checks rate limit in Redis (ElastiCache, cluster mode, 3 shards)
   - Routes to downstream microservice via internal ALB
6. Downstream services:
   - user-service: reads/writes to Aurora PostgreSQL (writer + 2 readers)
   - order-service: reads/writes to DynamoDB (on-demand capacity)
   - notification-service: publishes to SNS topic, which fans out to SQS queues
   - search-service: queries OpenSearch cluster (3 master + 6 data nodes)
7. All services emit traces to X-Ray and logs to CloudWatch
8. DynamoDB streams trigger Lambda function for order analytics → writes to Redshift

Here are two versions of the same function. Analyze the differences and their implications.

Version A:
def process_records(records, batch_size=100):
    results = []
    for i in range(0, len(records), batch_size):
        batch = records[i:i+batch_size]
        processed = [transform(r) for r in batch if validate(r)]
        results.extend(processed)
    return results

Version B:
def process_records(records, batch_size=100):
    results = []
    for i in range(0, len(records), batch_size):
        batch = records[i:i+batch_size]
        validated = [r for r in batch if validate(r)]
        processed = [transform(r) for r in validated]
        results.extend(processed)
    return results

Questions:
1. Do these two versions produce identical output for all possible inputs? Why or why not?
2. Which version is easier to debug and why?
3. What happens if transform() has side effects — does the ordering of operations matter?
4. If validate() is expensive, which version is more efficient?
5. Suggest a third version using a generator that would be more memory-efficient for large datasets.

Source A (Encyclopedia entry):
The Great Molasses Flood occurred on January 15, 1919, in Boston's North End neighborhood. A storage tank owned by Purity Distilling Company burst, releasing 2.3 million gallons of molasses in a wave estimated at 25 feet high traveling at 35 mph. The disaster killed 21 people and injured approximately 150. The tank, which was 50 feet tall and 90 feet in diameter, had been filled to near capacity. Structural failure was attributed to inadequate construction — the steel was too thin and the tank had never been properly tested.

Source B (Newspaper retrospective):
On a warm January day in 1919 — temperatures had risen unusually to 40°F after days of cold — a massive molasses tank in Boston's waterfront district collapsed. The resulting flood killed 21 people and injured over 150 others. Approximately 2.3 million gallons of crude molasses surged through the streets at an estimated 35 mph. The wave reached heights of 15 to 40 feet depending on location. Investigators later found that the tank had been poorly riveted and that the company had ignored reports of leaking.

Source C (Historical analysis):
The Boston Molasses Disaster of January 15, 1919, resulted from the catastrophic failure of a storage tank containing approximately 2.3 million gallons of molasses. The event claimed 21 lives and injured around 150. Recent engineering analyses suggest the wave moved at approximately 35 mph initially but slowed rapidly. The tank failure has been attributed to multiple factors: inadequate steel thickness (the walls were 0.667 inches at the base when they should have been at least 0.937 inches), temperature-related pressure changes as the day warmed, and possible fermentation of the molasses producing carbon dioxide gas that increased internal pressure.

Questions:

2. Where do the sources disagree about the wave height?
3. What different explanations are given for the tank failure?

- PROCESSING → OUT_OF_STOCK → REFUNDED or → BACKORDERED → PROCESSING (when restocked)
- SHIPPED → RETURNED → REFUND_REQUESTED → REFUNDED
- DELIVERED → RETURN_REQUESTED (within 30 days) → RETURNED → REFUND_REQUESTED → REFUNDED
- DELIVERED → COMPLETED (after 30 days, automatic)
- Any state except COMPLETED/REFUNDED → CANCELLED (by admin only)

Timeouts:
- PAYMENT_PENDING: auto-cancel after 30 minutes
- BACKORDERED: auto-cancel after 14 days
- RETURN_REQUESTED: must ship return within 7 days or request expires

Scenarios:
1. A customer places an order, pays successfully, the item is in stock, shipped, and received. After 15 days they request a return. Trace all states.
2. A customer places an order, payment fails twice, then succeeds on the third attempt. The item is out of stock and gets restocked after 10 days. Trace all states.
3. A customer places an order but never completes payment. What happens?
4. A customer receives their order and does nothing for 45 days. What is the final state?
5. Can a BACKORDERED order be cancelled by the customer (not admin)?

Below is a dialogue between two negotiators. Identify the tactics used.

Alex: We'd like to license your patent portfolio for $2 million per year.

Jordan: That's interesting, but our portfolio generated $8 million in licensing revenue last year from other clients. We couldn't go below $5 million.

Alex: $5 million is quite steep. You know, our company is the market leader — licensing to us gives you credibility that makes your other deals easier. That has value too.

Jordan: We appreciate that, but credibility doesn't pay our engineers. However, I'll tell you what — if you commit to a 5-year term instead of annual, we could look at $3.8 million per year.

Alex: Five years is a long commitment. What if the technology becomes obsolete? How about 3 years at $3 million?

Jordan: I need to be transparent — we have another company interested at $4.2 million for 3 years. I'm giving you the first opportunity because we'd prefer to work with a market leader, but I can't hold that offer forever.

Alex: We'd need to verify that. Tell you what — $3.5 million for 4 years, with a performance review clause at year 2 that allows either party to renegotiate.

Jordan: That's close. Make it $3.7 million with the review clause, and we have a deal.

Alex: $3.6 million, final offer.

Jordan: Done.

Questions:
1. What negotiation tactic does Alex use in the third exchange ('our company is the market leader')?
2. What tactic does Jordan use when mentioning 'another company interested at $4.2M'?
3. How did Jordan create movement from $5M to $3.8M?
4. What creative term did Alex introduce to manage risk?
5. What was the final agreed price, and how does it compare to each side's opening position?

Below is a complete Makefile. Explain the build process and answer questions.

```makefile
CC = gcc
CFLAGS = -Wall -Wextra -O2 -std=c17
LDFLAGS = -lm -lpthread
SRC_DIR = src
BUILD_DIR = build
BIN = $(BUILD_DIR)/app

SRCS = $(wildcard $(SRC_DIR)/*.c)
OBJS = $(SRCS:$(SRC_DIR)/%.c=$(BUILD_DIR)/%.o)
DEPS = $(OBJS:.o=.d)

.PHONY: all clean test install

all: $(BIN)

$(BIN): $(OBJS)
	@mkdir -p $(BUILD_DIR)
	$(CC) $(OBJS) -o $@ $(LDFLAGS)

$(BUILD_DIR)/%.o: $(SRC_DIR)/%.c
	@mkdir -p $(BUILD_DIR)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -MMD -MP -c $< -o $@

-include $(DEPS)

test: $(BIN)
	./$(BIN) --self-test
	python3 tests/integration.py

install: $(BIN)
	cp $(BIN) /usr/local/bin/app
	chmod 755 /usr/local/bin/app

clean:
	rm -rf $(BUILD_DIR)

print-%:
	@echo $* = $($*)
```

Below is a mock interview transcript. Evaluate the candidate's responses.

Interviewer: Can you walk me through a system you designed that handles high throughput?

Candidate: Sure. At my last company, we built an event processing pipeline that handled about 500,000 events per second. We used Kafka for ingestion with 120 partitions across 3 brokers. Consumers were written in Go, running 40 instances across 10 machines. Each consumer batch-processed events in groups of 1,000 and wrote to a Cassandra cluster with RF=3.

Interviewer: What happened when a consumer crashed mid-batch?

Candidate: We used manual offset commits, so if a consumer crashed, the next one would pick up from the last committed offset. That meant we could get duplicate processing for up to 1,000 events. We handled that with idempotent writes to Cassandra using the event ID as the primary key.

Interviewer: What about ordering guarantees?

Candidate: Events for the same entity were always routed to the same partition using the entity ID as the partition key, so per-entity ordering was guaranteed within a partition. We didn't need global ordering.

Interviewer: How did you monitor this system?

Candidate: We had Grafana dashboards tracking consumer lag, processing latency p50/p95/p99, error rates, and Cassandra write latency. We alerted on consumer lag exceeding 100,000 events and on error rate above 0.1%.

Interviewer: If you had to redesign this system today, what would you change?

Candidate: Honestly, I'd probably stick with a similar architecture. Kafka + Go consumers worked well. Maybe I'd consider using Kafka Streams or Flink instead of custom consumers to get exactly-once semantics out of the box. Also, I'd add dead letter queues for poison messages — we didn't have that initially and it caused a couple of incidents.

Questions:
1. What are the strongest aspects of the candidate's responses?
2. What follow-up question would expose whether they deeply understand Kafka?
3. Is their approach to handling consumer crashes sound? What edge case remains?
4. What did their answer to 'what would you change' reveal about their experience?
5. Rate this candidate (junior/mid/senior/staff) based solely on this transcript.

Here are 5 different map projections with their properties. Match each to its best use case.

Projection A: Mercator
  Shape: Preserved locally (conformal)
  Area: Greatly distorted (Greenland appears as large as Africa)
  Direction: Preserved (rhumb lines are straight)
  Distance: Distorted, especially near poles
  Special: Cannot show the poles

Projection B: Robinson
  Shape: Moderately distorted
  Area: Moderately distorted
  Direction: Not preserved
  Distance: Moderately distorted
  Special: Compromise projection — nothing is perfectly preserved but everything looks 'reasonable'

Projection C: Lambert Conformal Conic
  Shape: Preserved (conformal)
  Area: Minimal distortion along standard parallels
  Direction: Preserved near standard parallels
  Distance: Accurate along standard parallels
  Special: Best for mid-latitude regions with east-west extent

Projection D: Mollweide
  Shape: Distorted, especially at edges
  Area: Preserved exactly (equal-area)
  Direction: Not preserved
  Distance: Not preserved
  Special: Entire world shown as an ellipse

Projection E: Azimuthal Equidistant
  Shape: Distorted away from center
  Area: Distorted away from center
  Direction: True from center point only
  Distance: Preserved from center to any other point
  Special: Used by the UN flag; useful for showing distances from one city

Use cases:
1. A wall poster showing relative sizes of countries accurately for a geography classroom.
2. A nautical chart for sailing from New York to London.
3. An aviation map showing all points within 5,000 miles of Tokyo.
4. A weather map of the continental United States.
5. A general-purpose world atlas for a textbook.

Match each use case to the best projection and explain why.
